teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Non est factum ('it is not my deed') is a defence that allows a person who has signed a document to deny that the document is their deed. It is a plea of last resort — available only in narrow circumstances where the signer was, through no fault of their own, deceived into signing a document radically different from what they believed they were signing.

**The Origin:** The defence originated in *Foster v Mackinnon* (1869) LR 4 CP 704, where an elderly gentleman signed a guarantee believing it was a mere form. The document was in fact a bill of exchange for 3,000 pounds. The court held the signature was not his deed — non est factum — because he had been deceived as to the nature of the document.

**The Modern Test:** *Saunders v Anglia Building Society* [1971] AC 1004 (also known as *Gallie v Lee*) is the House of Lords' definitive modern restatement. The test is strict: (1) the signer must have signed through no carelessness of their own, and (2) the document must be radically different from what they believed. A mere difference in the legal effect is insufficient.

**A Plea of Last Resort:** Non est factum is not available merely because a document is disadvantageous or was not read. It applies where the signer's mind did not accompany the signature — where the document is essentially different in character or content from what was represented.

**In short:** Non est factum is an exceptional defence for those deceived into signing a document fundamentally different from what they thought. Carelessness bars the plea. It does not protect those who simply failed to read what they signed.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004
Foster v Mackinnon (1869) LR 4 CP 704

question: The modern authority on non est factum is __________ v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004, in which the House of Lords set out the strict test for the defence.
feedback: Correct. *Saunders v Anglia Building Society* [1971] AC 1004 (also reported as *Gallie v Lee* [1971] AC 1004) is the leading authority on non est factum.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Non est factum is a __________ law defence. It is not codified in statute but has been developed through the cases from *Foster v Mackinnon* (1869) to *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971].
feedback: Correct. Non est factum is a creature of the common law — it has no statutory basis. Its scope and limits are defined exclusively by judicial decision.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Arrange the requirements for a successful plea of non est factum under *Saunders* [1971] in logical order:
answer: The signer must have signed the document → The signer must show they were deceived as to its nature or content → The document must be radically different from what the signer believed → The signer must not have been careless in signing → The plea succeeds — the signature is not the signer's deed

options.text: The signer must have signed the document
The signer must show they were deceived as to its nature or content
The document must be radically different from what the signer believed
The signer must not have been careless in affixing their signature
If all satisfied, the plea succeeds — the signature is not the signer's deed

feedback: Correct. The *Saunders* [1971] test is cumulative. The 'radically different' requirement and the 'no carelessness' bar are the two principal hurdles.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: An elderly woman, Mrs Cole, is visited by her nephew who asks her to sign 'a form to help with his tax return.' She signs without reading. The document is actually a guarantee of the nephew's 50,000 pound bank loan. The bank sues Mrs Cole. She pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971]:

options.text: Mrs Cole succeeds — she was deceived by her nephew as to the document's nature
options.feedback: The question is whether she was careless. She had the opportunity to read and chose not to.

options.text: Mrs Cole succeeds — a guarantee is radically different from a tax return form
options.feedback: Radical difference is strong here, but it is not enough alone. The carelessness bar must also be considered.

options.text: Mrs Cole is likely to fail — her failure to read the document constitutes carelessness barring the plea, even if the document was radically different
options.feedback: Correct. Under *Saunders* [1971], a signer who could read and had the opportunity to do so but chose not to is likely to be careless. The plea is barred.

options.text: Mrs Cole succeeds because she is elderly and was deceived by a family member
options.feedback: Age and familial relationship are not defences. The test is carelessness — did she take reasonable care before signing?

feedback: The 'radically different' requirement and the carelessness bar work together. A signer who can read but chooses not to is generally barred. Blind trust in another is not sufficient — that is the very carelessness the doctrine guards against.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'Non est factum is available to anyone who signs a document without reading it. If a person simply trusts someone else and signs, they can later claim the document is not their deed and avoid all liability.'
answer: Non est factum is not available to those who are careless. A person who can read and chooses not to, or who signs without inquiry, will be barred. The defence does not protect the merely careless or the wilfully blind.
feedback: Found it. *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971] — the carelessness bar is central. Lord Reid: 'The plea cannot be available to a person whose failure to read the document is attributable to his own carelessness.' Signing without reading, without good reason, is the classic example.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Which THREE of the following are required for a successful plea of non est factum under *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971]?

options.text: The document signed was radically different from what the signer believed
options.feedback: Correct. Lord Wilberforce: 'a radical difference between what was signed and what was thought to be signed.'

options.text: The signer must have received independent legal advice before signing
options.feedback: Independent legal advice is not a requirement — though its absence may be relevant to carelessness.

options.text: The signer must not have been careless in affixing their signature
options.feedback: Correct. Carelessness is an absolute bar to the plea.

options.text: The document must have been signed under physical duress
options.feedback: Non est factum concerns deception as to the nature of the document — not duress, which is a separate vitiating factor.

options.text: The signer's mind must not have accompanied the signature — the plea is one of last resort
options.feedback: Correct. Lord Pearson: 'the plea should be kept within narrow limits.'

feedback: Correct answers: (a), (c), (e). The *Saunders* [1971] test has three essential elements: radical difference, absence of carelessness, and absence of the signer's mind. The plea is deliberately narrow.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: The earliest authority for non est factum is __________ v Mackinnon (1869) LR 4 CP 704, where a signature was obtained by deception as to the nature of the document.
feedback: Correct. *Foster v Mackinnon* (1869) — the signer thought he was witnessing an insurance document; it was actually a bill of exchange. The signature was held to be not his deed.
oscoaReferences: Foster v Mackinnon (1869) LR 4 CP 704

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** *Saunders v Anglia Building Society* [1971] AC 1004 — also known as *Gallie v Lee* — is the House of Lords' landmark decision that narrowed and modernised the defence of non est factum. Their Lordships held that the defence must be kept within strict bounds so as not to undermine the reliability of signed documents in commercial life.

**The Facts:** Mrs Gallie, a widow aged 78, owned a house with no mortgage. She was persuaded by her nephew's friend, Lee, to sign a document that she was told was a deed of gift of the house to her nephew. In fact, the document was an assignment of the house to Lee for 3,000 pounds — which Lee used as security for a loan from Anglia Building Society. Mrs Gallie had broken her glasses and could not read the document. She signed. Lee defaulted on the loan, and the building society sought possession.

**The Decision:** The House of Lords unanimously rejected Mrs Gallie's plea of non est factum. The document she signed (an assignment of the house) was not radically different from what she thought she was signing (a gift of the house to her nephew). Both involved transferring the house. The difference was in the transferee and the consideration — matters of legal effect, not fundamental character. Her failure to obtain new glasses or have the document read to her constituted carelessness.

**Lord Wilberforce's Test:** A person who signs a document and does not take the trouble to read it, but relies on another's word, cannot plead non est factum unless the document is 'radically' or 'fundamentally' or 'totally' different from what was described.

**In short:** *Saunders* [1971] confirms that non est factum protects the blind and illiterate who cannot discover the nature of the document by reasonable means — not those who simply choose not to read.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004
Gallie v Lee [1971] AC 1004

question: In Saunders v Anglia BS [1971], Mrs __________ signed an assignment believing it was a deed of gift — the House of Lords held the document was not radically different and the plea failed.
feedback: Correct. The case is reported under two names: *Saunders v Anglia Building Society* and *Gallie v Lee* [1971] AC 1004. Mrs Gallie was the elderly homeowner whose plea was rejected.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004
Gallie v Lee [1971] AC 1004

question: The difference must be '__________', '__________' or '__________' — a mere difference in legal effect is not enough for non est factum (*Saunders* [1971]).
feedback: Correct. Lord Wilberforce used all three adjectives. A difference in legal effect (different transferee, different price) is insufficient — the document must be essentially different in character.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Place the facts of *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971] in chronological order:
answer: Mrs Gallie (78) owned a mortgage-free house → Lee (nephew's friend) asked her to sign a document → Lee said it was a deed of gift to her nephew → Mrs Gallie signed — her glasses were broken, she could not read → The document was actually an assignment to Lee for 3,000 pounds → Lee borrowed money from the building society using the house as security → Lee defaulted; building society sought possession → House of Lords unanimously rejected non est factum

options.text: Mrs Gallie (78) owned a house free of mortgage
Lee (a friend of her nephew) asked her to sign a document
Lee said the document was a deed of gift to her nephew
Mrs Gallie signed — her glasses were broken, she could not read it
The document was actually an assignment of the house to Lee for 3,000 pounds
Lee borrowed money from Anglia BS using the house as security
Lee defaulted; the building society sought possession
House of Lords unanimously rejected Mrs Gallie's non est factum plea

feedback: Correct. The key facts: Mrs Gallie's broken glasses (she could not read), the document's true nature (assignment, not gift), and her failure to take alternative steps (borrowing glasses, asking someone else to read it).
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: David (45, literate) is asked by his friend to sign a document described as 'a reference letter for my job application.' David signs without reading. The document is a personal guarantee of his friend's 30,000 pound loan. The bank sues David, who pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders* [1971]:

options.text: David succeeds — a guarantee is radically different from a reference letter
options.feedback: The document is radically different, but David must also be free of carelessness.

options.text: David succeeds because he was deceived by a friend
options.feedback: Deception alone is not enough; the carelessness bar applies regardless of who deceived the signer.

options.text: David succeeds because his mind did not accompany the signature
options.feedback: But why didn't his mind accompany the signature? Because he chose not to read. That choice is carelessness.

options.text: David fails — he is literate, could read the document, and chose not to. His carelessness bars the plea of non est factum, even though the document was radically different
options.feedback: Correct. David is the classic case of carelessness: a literate person who chooses not to read. *Saunders* [1971] makes clear that such a person cannot rely on non est factum. He must bear the consequences of his own failure to check.

feedback: David's scenario highlights the carelessness bar. Unlike Mrs Gallie (whose broken glasses provided some explanation), David had no impediment. A literate person who signs without reading a readily readable document is almost certainly careless. The plea fails.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question:
Spot the legal error:

'If a document is radically different from what the signer believed, the plea of non est factum will always succeed. The carelessness of the signer is irrelevant — what matters is whether the document was fundamentally misrepresented.'
answer: Carelessness is an **absolute bar** to non est factum. Even if the document is radically different, a careless signer cannot rely on the defence. The plea is a last resort for those who could not have discovered the truth, not for those who chose not to look.
feedback: Found it. *Saunders* [1971] — Lord Wilberforce emphasised that the signer must have taken reasonable care. A person who could have discovered the document's true nature by ordinary care cannot plead non est factum. The carelessness bar is independent of the radical difference test — both must be satisfied.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: In *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971], which TWO factors were decisive in the House of Lords' unanimous rejection of Mrs Gallie's non est factum plea?

options.text: Mrs Gallie was elderly and the court gave her the benefit of the doubt
options.feedback: Mrs Gallie actually lost — age was not a decisive factor in her favour.

options.text: The document (assignment) was not radically different from what she believed (deed of gift) — both transferred the house
options.feedback: Correct. Lord Wilberforce: the difference between assignment and gift went to legal effect, not fundamental character. The essential nature (transfer of the house) was the same.

options.text: The building society was negligent in accepting the assignment
options.feedback: The building society's conduct was not the basis of the decision. The Lords focused on Mrs Gallie's own conduct and the nature of the document.

options.text: Mrs Gallie was careless — she could have borrowed glasses or asked someone to read the document to her
options.feedback: Correct. Despite her broken glasses, Mrs Gallie could have taken alternative steps. Her failure to do so was carelessness, barring the plea.

options.text: Mrs Gallie was found to have known the true nature of the document all along
options.feedback: The House of Lords accepted Mrs Gallie's evidence that she did not know. The issue was not her honesty but the carelessness and the lack of radical difference.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). The two reasons were: (1) the document was not radically different (assignment vs gift of the same house), and (2) Mrs Gallie was careless in not finding a way to read the document. Both bars independently defeated the plea.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Ahmed is blind and signs a document that his carer tells him is 'a form to register for disability benefits.' It is in fact a contract to sell Ahmed's house to the carer for 10,000 pounds. Ahmed cannot read at all. The market value of the house is 200,000. Ahmed pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders* [1971]:

options.text: Ahmed fails — all signers are expected to read what they sign
options.feedback: A blind person cannot read. The carelessness test is adjusted to the individual's capacities. *Saunders* [1971] — the question is what a reasonable person with the same disability would do.

options.text: Ahmed is likely to succeed — a contract to sell his house is radically different from a benefits form, and as a blind person who relied on his carer, he may not have been careless
options.feedback: Correct. A house sale contract is completely different in character from a benefits registration form. Ahmed's blindness means he had no means to verify the document himself. Reliance on a carer in such circumstances may not be careless.

options.text: Ahmed fails because his carer's fraud is a matter between them — the contract stands
options.feedback: Non est factum applies precisely to cases where the signer is deceived by another. The fraud does not bar the defence — it is the premise of it.

options.text: Ahmed succeeds automatically because he is blind and the document was misrepresented
options.feedback: Blindness alone does not guarantee success. The court will ask whether Ahmed acted reasonably — e.g. did he ask someone else to confirm, could he have sought independent advice?

feedback: The carelessness standard is subjective to the signer's capacities. A blind person cannot read; reliance on a trusted person may be reasonable. The radical difference (benefits form vs house sale) is clear. This is the kind of case where non est factum may succeed — unlike the careless literate signer.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Non est factum is a plea of last resort — a narrow defence that operates as an exception to the general rule that a person who signs a document is bound by its terms. The rationale is the need to protect those who, through no fault of their own, cannot appreciate what they are signing, while preserving the certainty and reliability of signed documents in commerce.

**Who Can Plead It:** The defence is primarily for the blind, the illiterate, and those who, by reason of a disability or the circumstances of the transaction, could not reasonably have appreciated the nature of the document. A person who can read, or could have asked someone to read, is normally barred by carelessness.

**The Carer / Interpreter Exception:** Where a signer must rely on a third party to explain a document (because of blindness, illiteracy, or language barrier), it is not careless to rely on that person — provided the reliance was reasonable in the circumstances. But if the signer could easily have verified with an independent person and did not, the plea may still fail.

**Relationship to Other Doctrines:** Non est factum overlaps with but is distinct from:
- **Misrepresentation:** Focuses on a false statement inducing the contract. Non est factum goes further — it denies the signature altogether.
- **Undue Influence:** Focuses on pressure from a relationship of trust. Non est factum addresses the nature of the document, not the quality of consent.
- **Duress:** Focuses on coercion. Non est factum addresses deception about what is being signed.

**In short:** Non est factum is the nuclear option of vitiating factors — it destroys the signature itself. Courts grant it sparingly because of its drastic effect on third parties who rely on signed documents.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004
Foster v Mackinnon (1869) LR 4 CP 704
Lloyds Bank v Waterhouse [1993] 2 FLR 97

question: In __________ Bank v Waterhouse [1993] 2 FLR 97, a father who signed a guarantee believing it was for a limited amount failed in his non est factum plea — he could read and chose not to, and the document was not radically different.
feedback: Correct. *Lloyds Bank v Waterhouse* [1993] — the signer could read but did not bother. The Court of Appeal applied *Saunders* [1971] and rejected the plea. The difference was one of extent, not nature.
oscoaReferences: Lloyds Bank v Waterhouse [1993] 2 FLR 97

question: Non est factum renders a document __________ ab initio — as if the signature was never the signer's. This is distinct from voidability, which allows affirmation.
feedback: Correct. Like common mistake, non est factum goes to the root — the signature is treated as never having been made. The document is void, not voidable. Third parties who relied on the signed document are at risk (unlike voidable contracts where *bona fide* purchasers may be protected).
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Match each vitiating factor to its core focus:
answer: Non Est Factum=The nature and character of the document — 'it is not my deed'; Misrepresentation=False statement of fact inducing the contract; Undue Influence=Improper pressure from a relationship of trust or ascendancy; Duress=Coerced consent through unlawful threat or pressure

options.text: The nature and character of the document — 'it is not my deed'
False statement of fact that induced the contract
Improper pressure arising from a relationship of trust or ascendancy
Coerced consent through an unlawful threat or illegitimate pressure

feedback: Well matched. Non est factum is unique: it denies the signature itself. Misrepresentation and duress render a contract voidable; non est factum renders it void from the start (if successful).
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Elena, a Russian speaker with limited English, signs a document her employer says is 'an updated employment contract with the same terms.' She cannot read English well. The document is actually a compromise agreement waiving all her employment rights, including a pending discrimination claim worth 50,000 pounds. Elena pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders* [1971]:

options.text: Elena fails — language barriers are never relevant to non est factum
options.feedback: Incorrect. Language barriers are relevant to carelessness. A person who cannot read the language cannot be expected to verify the document personally.

options.text: Elena automatically succeeds because the document was not in her native language
options.feedback: A non-English speaker is not automatically excused. The court will ask whether Elena acted reasonably — e.g. did she ask for a translation, could she have sought independent advice?

options.text: Elena has a strong argument — a waiver of all rights is radically different from a contract on the same terms, and her language barrier may excuse her from carelessness if her reliance on the employer was reasonable
options.feedback: Correct. A compromise agreement extinguishing employment rights is radically different in character from an updated contract retaining those rights. Elena's language barrier is relevant to carelessness — she could not verify by reading. However, the court will also ask whether she should have sought independent advice or a translation.

options.text: Elena fails because she should have learned English before signing legal documents
options.feedback: The law does not require signers to learn the language of the contract. The question is whether Elena acted reasonably given her level of English.

feedback: Language barriers raise similar issues to blindness or illiteracy. The signer must act reasonably given their capacities. A waiver of rights is radically different from a contract maintaining rights. But Elena may need to show why she did not seek independent advice or ask for a translation — the employer's representation alone may not be enough if she could easily have verified.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question:
Spot the legal error:

'Non est factum and misrepresentation are the same thing. Both involve being told something false about a document, and both make the contract voidable. The remedies are identical, and there is no practical difference between pleading one or the other.'
answer: Non est factum and misrepresentation are **distinct**. Non est factum denies the signature itself (document is void). Misrepresentation makes a contract voidable (at the innocent party's election). The consequence for third parties is fundamentally different.
feedback: Found it. Non est factum (void) vs misrepresentation (voidable) is a critical distinction. A successfully pleaded non est factum means the document was never the signer's deed — title never passes, and third parties cannot acquire good title. Misrepresentation gives the innocent party an election to rescind, but third-party rights may intervene.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Which TWO of the following correctly describe the scope and limits of non est factum after *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971]?

options.text: It is a broad defence available to anyone disadvantaged by a signed document
options.feedback: No — it is a narrow plea of last resort, not a general safety net for bad bargains.

options.text: It is primarily for the blind, illiterate, or those who cannot appreciate the document's nature through no fault of their own
options.feedback: Correct. The defence is calibrated to protect those who cannot, rather than will not, understand what they are signing.

options.text: The signer's carelessness is irrelevant — only the radical difference in the document matters
options.feedback: Incorrect. Carelessness is an absolute bar. Both radical difference and absence of carelessness must be proved.

options.text: If successful, the document is void ab initio — the signature is treated as never having been the signer's
options.feedback: Correct. Non est factum renders the document void — this is what distinguishes it from misrepresentation (voidable) and makes it so drastic.

options.text: A signer who is illiterate or blind will always succeed in a plea of non est factum
options.feedback: Incorrect. The signer must still show the document was radically different and that they acted reasonably. Blindness is relevant but not determinative.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). Non est factum is a narrow, drastic defence. It protects those who cannot appreciate the document, not those who choose not to. If successful, the document is void — a consequence so severe that courts reserve it for truly exceptional cases.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: Match each scenario to whether non est factum is likely to succeed or fail:
answer: Blind person signs a house sale believing it to be a benefits form=Succeed — radically different, no carelessness if reliance on carer was reasonable; Literate businessperson signs without reading=Fail — carelessness bars the plea; Elderly person with broken glasses signs a deed of gift that is an assignment=Fail — not radically different (both transfer house); Illiterate person signs guarantee believing it is a receipt=Succeed — radically different, illiteracy excuses from reading

options.text: Blind person signs a house sale believing it is a benefits form
options.pair: Succeed — radically different, no carelessness if reliance was reasonable

options.text: Literate businessperson signs a guarantee without reading it
Elderly person with broken glasses signs a deed of gift that is an assignment
options.pair: Fail — not radically different (both transfer the house)

options.text: Illiterate person signs a guarantee believing it is a receipt
options.pair: Succeed — radically different, illiteracy excuses reading

feedback: Well matched. Each scenario tests one element: radical difference (s3 vs s1/s4), carelessness (s2 vs s1), or the protected class (s1, s4 vs s2, s3).
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004
Foster v Mackinnon (1869) LR 4 CP 704

question: PC, a 90-year-old partially-sighted man, is told by his son to sign 'some papers to help with the family business.' PC signs. The papers are a personal guarantee of the son's 500,000 pound business loan. PC cannot read the small print and trusts his son. The bank seeks to enforce the guarantee. Applying *Saunders* [1971], will PC's non est factum plea succeed?

options.text: Yes — PC is elderly and partially sighted, and non est factum is designed for precisely this situation
options.feedback: Age and partial sight alone do not guarantee success. The court will examine PC's actual capacities and what steps he could reasonably have taken.

options.text: It depends — the guarantee is radically different from 'papers to help the family business,' but the court will assess whether PC could have asked someone else to read them or sought independent advice
options.feedback: Correct. The radical difference is arguable, but the carelessness analysis is complex. PC's partial sight means he may need assistance — but relying solely on the interested son (the beneficiary of the guarantee) may be unreasonable. The court will balance his disability against the availability of independent verification.

options.text: No — PC signed it and is therefore bound regardless
options.feedback: Incorrect. The law does recognise non est factum. The signature is the starting point, but the defence can displace it.

options.text: No — bank guarantees are never radically different from family business papers
options.feedback: A 500,000 pound personal guarantee is arguably radically different in character from 'papers to help the business' — the issue is the carelessness element, not the radical difference.

feedback: This is the borderline case. Radical difference: probably satisfied (a 500,000 pound guarantee is fundamentally different from administrative papers). But carelessness: PC relied on his son — the very person who would benefit. The court may find PC should have asked an independent person (e.g. a solicitor or another family member) to verify. The plea is not hopeless, but it is far from certain.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block A] Pedro, a Spanish speaker who cannot read English, is handed a document by his landlord who says 'sign here to confirm you received the keys.' Pedro signs. The document is a deed of guarantee for the landlord's business loan. The landlord defaults and the bank sues Pedro. Pedro pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders* [1971]:

options.text: Pedro fails because he signed — all signatures bind the signer
options.feedback: Incorrect. Non est factum displaces the signature rule in exceptional circumstances.

options.text: Pedro has a strong case — a guarantee is radically different from a key receipt, and his inability to read English may excuse him from carelessness if his reliance on the landlord was not unreasonable
options.feedback: Correct. The document is radically different in character. Pedro cannot read English, so personal verification is impossible. The question is whether relying on the landlord was reasonable — if the landlord was his only source of information and no independent help was readily available, the plea may succeed.

options.text: Pedro fails because he should have learned English
options.feedback: There is no duty to learn English to sign documents. The carelessness standard is adjusted to the signer's actual capacities.

options.text: Pedro automatically succeeds because of the language barrier
options.feedback: The carelessness analysis applies — the court will ask whether Pedro acted reasonably. A language barrier helps but is not an automatic defence.

feedback: This consolidates Block A. The key elements are: (1) radical difference (key receipt vs guarantee — clearly satisfied) and (2) carelessness (language barrier means Pedro cannot read — reliance on the landlord may be reasonable, but the court will ask whether he should have sought independent help).
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] In Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971], Mrs __________ failed in her non est factum plea because the document (assignment) was not radically different from what she expected (deed of gift).
feedback: Correct. *Saunders v Anglia BS* [1971] — also known as *Gallie v Lee*. Mrs Gallie lost because the difference was one of legal effect (transferee and consideration), not fundamental character. Both documents transferred the house.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] James, a 35-year-old accountant, signs a document his colleague says is 'the minutes of last week's meeting.' In fact it is a personal loan guarantee. James did not read it because he was in a hurry. He pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders* [1971]:

options.text: James succeeds — a loan guarantee is radically different from meeting minutes
options.feedback: The document may be radically different, but the carelessness bar is the problem.

options.text: James succeeds because he was deceived by his colleague
options.feedback: Deception does not excuse carelessness. The two elements are independent.

options.text: James succeeds because being in a hurry is a reasonable excuse for not reading
options.feedback: Being in a hurry is not a reasonable excuse — it is the very carelessness the doctrine penalises.

options.text: James fails — as a literate professional who could have read the document, his failure to do so is carelessness and bars the plea
options.feedback: Correct. James is the classic careless signer. He could read, had opportunity, and chose not to because he was busy. The plea is barred regardless of whether the document was radically different.

feedback: James is the mirror image of a successful non est factum claimant. Literate, capable, no disability — just a failure to take reasonable care. *Saunders* [1971] is clear: such a person cannot invoke non est factum.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] Which TWO of the following correctly state the law of non est factum after *Saunders* [1971]?

options.text: The document must be radically, fundamentally or totally different from what the signer believed
options.feedback: Correct. Lord Wilberforce's triple test — the difference must go to the essential character of the document.

options.text: The defence is available to anyone who did not read the document, regardless of why
options.feedback: Incorrect. Those who could have read but chose not to are barred by carelessness.

options.text: A successful plea makes the contract voidable at the signer's election
options.feedback: Incorrect. Non est factum renders the document void — not voidable. This is a fundamental distinction.

options.text: Illiterate and blind signers are automatically protected by non est factum
options.feedback: Incorrect. Their disability is relevant to carelessness but does not guarantee success. The document must still be radically different.

options.text: The signer must not have been careless — reasonable steps must have been taken to verify the document's nature
options.feedback: Correct. The carelessness bar applies to all signers. What is 'reasonable' depends on the signer's capacities and circumstances.

feedback: Correct answers: (a) and (e). The two core elements are radical difference and absence of carelessness. Both must be proved. If successful, the document is void ab initio. The defence is narrow, exceptional, and granted sparingly.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block C] Fatima, who is both blind and illiterate in English, signs a document her interpreter tells her is 'an application for housing benefit.' The document is a tenancy agreement with a monthly rent of 2,000 pounds — far more than Fatima can afford. The landlord seeks to enforce. Fatima pleads non est factum. Applying *Saunders* [1971]:

options.text: Fatima has a strong case — a tenancy agreement at 2,000 pounds/month is radically different from a benefits application, and as a blind, illiterate person she had no means to verify the document herself. If her reliance on the interpreter was reasonable, the plea may succeed
options.feedback: Correct. Fatima's combined disabilities mean she could not verify the document at all. The radical difference is clear. The court will consider whether she could reasonably have obtained verification from an independent source. If not, the plea succeeds.

options.text: Fatima fails — everyone is expected to verify documents before signing
options.feedback: A blind, illiterate person cannot verify documents. The carelessness standard is adjusted to capacity — Fatima is not careless if she could not verify and her reliance was reasonable.

options.text: Fatima fails because the interpreter's deception is a separate matter
options.feedback: The interpreter's deception is the very basis of the non est factum plea — the signer was deceived as to the nature of the document by a person they relied upon.

options.text: Fatima succeeds but only if the interpreter is convicted of fraud
options.feedback: The civil plea of non est factum does not depend on criminal proceedings against the fraudster.

feedback: Fatima represents the strongest case for non est factum: combined disabilities (blind and illiterate), radical difference (benefits application vs commercial tenancy), and reliance on a person who would reasonably be trusted (an interpreter). If there was no accessible independent verification, her reliance was not careless. This is where a non est factum plea is most likely to succeed.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004

question: [CONSOLIDATION — All Blocks] The Latin phrase non est __________ means 'it is not my deed' and is the name of the defence that allows a signer to deny their signature on a document.
feedback: Correct. Non est factum — literally 'it is not my deed.' The defence allows a signer to assert that, despite the appearance of their signature, the document is not their act.
oscoaReferences: Saunders v Anglia Building Society [1971] AC 1004
